dialogues.prompt: The coffee machine hums. The pot is half full. The pot has been half full for three hours.
Someone wrote 'pls clean me — humanity is counting on you' on a sticky note above the carafe. The note is older than the carafe.
The whiteboard has someone's half-erased system diagram. Whoever drew it gave up at the database layer.
Someone wrote 'YAGNI' in giant letters across the top of the board, three months ago, and nobody has erased it. I think it was Sharpie. *Sigh*
Your second monitor has an old Slack thread open. The thread is from a project that shipped six months ago.
Your screen has thirty tabs open. None of them are the one you were looking for.
The printer makes a sound like clearing its throat. It hasn't printed anything in eight months.
There's a sticky note on the printer that says 'IT KNOWS'. The note is taped over another note that says the same thing.
The plant in the corner is, against all reasonable expectation, still alive.
Someone watered the plant. The leaves look, somehow, suspicious about it.
The wall calendar shows three months ago. Someone has X'd through every day up to a Wednesday.
A wall calendar. Someone has circled a random day in November and written 'breathe'.
A stress ball on the desk. It has been squeezed past the point where it can really be called a ball.

options.label: Squeeze it
options.flavor: Three quick squeezes. Two long ones. Slightly better, very briefly.

options.label: Leave it
options.flavor: You walked past it. It will be there tomorrow.

dialogues.prompt: The water cooler jug is empty. The replacement jug is on the floor beside it, where it has been for a week.
Someone left their coffee mug on top of the cooler. The mug says 'WORLD'S OKAYEST DAD'.
The intern looks up. "Wait, is the standup canceled? Or does it just feel that way?"
The intern is reading a documentation page that doesn't exist. They're going to find out in about ten minutes.
The intern catches you in the hallway. "Got a sec? Quick question about the staging deploy."

options.label: Sure, walk and talk
options.flavor: Five minutes turns into fifteen. They had it almost right.

options.label: Maybe later
options.flavor: You say later. You both know later sometimes means never.

dialogues.prompt: An intern catches the name on your screen. "Stack Overflow — that's the one where they're all kinda mean to you, right? My professor showed us a screenshot once."
An intern watches you scroll. "Wait — you used to read the whole documentation? Before asking? Like, all of it??"
An intern peers over your shoulder. "Hold on. You fixed merge conflicts by hand? You looked at the <<<<<<< thing and just… picked one? With your eyes??"
An intern hears you say "PR review." Their eyes get wide. "Oh my god — another human read your code? And left comments? And you had to respond to them? Like a whole conversation?"
You mention googling something. The intern stares. "Wait, you used to Google it? Like type the question, get blue links, open six tabs, compare?"
The intern, mid-coffee: "My dad said his first job interview had a whiteboard. A real one. With a marker. While people watched. That's not an interview, that's a hazing ritual."
An intern watches you in a shell. "Sometimes my dad still types into the black window. He says it makes him feel powerful. I just ask the model."
An intern, squinting at your terminal: "What's a README? Oh — that's the thing the model reads for me, right?"
The intern survives their first standup. After: "You'd all stop work and stand up and say things? For how long? Couldn't the model just summarize?"
An intern watches you write a component. "You used to type out the whole thing? Every time? Even the boilerplate? With your hands?"
The intern leans over your shoulder. You're typing. They watch for a long beat. "You mean you have to use your hands? … That's like a baby's toy."
The senior engineer glances up from a 200-line stack trace. "It's always the cache."
The senior catches you in the kitchen. "Got a sec? I want to push back on a design call you made."

options.label: Hear them out
options.flavor: Their argument was harder to dismiss than you expected. You spent the afternoon redoing the part you'd just finished.

options.label: Promise to look at the docs later
options.flavor: You closed the conversation politely. The 'later' became 'never.' They knew.

dialogues.prompt: The PM types a status update with the focus of someone defusing a bomb.
The PM corners you with a smile. "Quick favor — can you scope this thing for me before Friday?"

options.label: Sure, send the link
options.flavor: It was three things. None of them fit on a page.

options.label: I'll let you know
options.flavor: You said next week. They said no rush — like everyone who says no rush.

dialogues.prompt: The designer adjusts a kerning value by 0.5 pixels and squints. "Better."
The designer hovers over a button color in Figma. They've been there for twenty minutes.
A coworker walks by. "Hey — did you see the staging incident? Yeah. It was the cache."
Your peer leans over the cubicle wall. "Wanna grab lunch?"

options.label: Yeah, let's go
options.flavor: Two hours later you're at your desk, still smiling at something they said.

options.label: Today's no good
options.flavor: They went without you. They didn't bring it up tomorrow.

dialogues.prompt: The new hire is looking at the org chart with the expression of someone solving a puzzle.
The new hire asks where the bathroom is. Then where the kitchen is. Then if you have time for coffee.

options.label: Make time
options.flavor: Twenty minutes. You answered every question. They sent a thank-you message at 6pm.

options.label: Some other day
options.flavor: You said next week. You both knew it meant later. Or never.

dialogues.prompt: Your skip-level walks past, gives you a small nod, keeps walking. The nod was, you think, encouraging.
The skip-level catches your eye. "Hey — got a minute to chat about what you're working on?"

options.label: Walk them through it
options.flavor: They listened. They asked one question. It was a good question.

options.label: Now's not great
options.flavor: They understood. They told three other people on their team they 'tried.'
